speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number six with Erwin Chaim on October 25, 1996.

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived in San Francisco and were immediately assigned quarters, hotel rooms, in various parts of San Francisco, which were sponsored by the Joint Committee for the Refugees, paid for. And we were also given some money to buy food every day, go to restaurants and buy-- and-- and eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was, at that time, 18 years old. And the first thing that happened was the fact that our hotel was at the corner of Powell and Market Street. It just so happened that the corner of Powell and Market Street-- Market Street is the end station of the famous cable car. And they had a turntable there. Well-- and we had a room that looked out directly over this cable car and the people turning the-- the car on the turntable, then going back up the hill, up Powell Street towards Fisherman's Wharf. And we spent about three weeks adjusting ourselves to the wonderful, wonderful life in the United States. We--

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your English at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bad, bad, bad. I spoke-- oh, I spoke perfect English, only no one understood what I was saying. And I, of course, couldn't understand them, either. But you get along. You get along in China, you can certainly get along in the United States. One of the things that happened, of course, was ice cream. Ice cream was unknown in-- in China. There was no facilities for that. Oh, boy-- ice cream. There is such a thing as a sundae. Well, we thought that was only-- you could only eat that on Sunday. That was why it was called sundae. We never asked for ice cream the rest of the week. You had to have the Sunday on sundae. These are the things, you know. We also decided real quick that the money that was given to us to eat in restaurants was really wasteful. We could go to a delicatessen store. We could go to a grocery store, smuggle the food into the hotel room, and have sandwiches and a bottle of Coke or beer-- my father liked beer-- much, much cheaper than being served in a restaurant. Well, this was strictly illegal, of course, and we didn't do any cooking. But we actually saved a lot of money that first month that we were in the United States just by supplying ourselves and eating in the hotel room, or-- or maybe in the park. You know. My enterprising father always knew all the angles.

speaker: interviewee
text: So finally, we went on a train and came to Denver, Colorado, where we were supposed to go, because our sponsor-- former Senator Hershfield-- lived. We got to the Union Station in Denver. And our good friends, the Michaelsons, met us in a rented car. My-- our good friends-- he knew how to drive, but he didn't own a car.

speaker: interviewee
text: Took us to an apartment on 1050 Logan Street, which was two rooms, was a share kitchen and a share bath. This is 1947, and things were still pretty scarce here. And apartments were quite square-- scarce. We only stayed there for about a month. Oh, I think we paid $90 a month. Whoa, a lot of money at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever meet Senator Hershfield?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. Oh yes. We-- we had a meeting with him and thanked him for his generosity of rescuing us from Shanghai. That was the end of that. He really didn't, you know, want anything to do with us at-- OK, I got you out of Shanghai. And now that's it. You know, just don't become a burden to me, which we didn't. And my father immediately tried to become a dentist here in the United States. And that was not allowed. Without going to college again, you can't practice dentistry. Well, as my-- so my father hired himself out as a dental technician. His particular brand of dentistry included making the dentures, also, besides doing fillings, [? stuff ?] like that. So he was a very accomplished dental technician. He worked for a-- a-- a dentist here in Denver, and-- a Jewish dentist, and basically was actually quite mistreated. This particular dentist always seemed to find ways of keeping my father there after the 5 o'clock going home time by having an impression poured and having to wait for that. And my father has always been a very independent person. And after a very short time, he says, up yours. Forget it. And he-- he-- My father always liked cars. And I-- as-- we-- we then lived on the west side in the basement apartment. The upstairs-- the people that rented to us were the [? Kirsteins. ?] We lived in the apartment downstairs. And my father decided he needed a car. He wanted a car. He didn't need one. He wanted a car. He bought himself a second hand car, and he opened citywide delivery service, which didn't do very well. He did deliver telephone books.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you help him with these things?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I had a job. I will call-- talk to that in about a minute. And then he-- he worked for the Allied campaign chartering cards-- charts back and forth. That didn't last very long. The main thing was he wanted a car. [LAUGHS] When I got to Shanghai-- I had a-- excuse me, when I got to Denver, the second day I was here, I had a-- an interview with the Community Chest. And they furnished me with a job the third day I was here. The thing that I learned to do best in Shanghai at that time was spray painting. So I became a spray painter for Quick Way Truck Shovel Company. And I got the job through the Community Chest. OK. I got there. The-- my foreman was a Texan who spoke Texan English, which was non-- not comparable at all with my kind of English. I was asked to spray paint a large, 10'-by-10' roof in black lacquer paint with a spray gun that weighed about 20 pounds with the paint included. Well, when I learned how to spray paint in Shanghai, we spray painted with a tiny little gun, and we had a coolie pumping manually the air into a reservoir of about maybe between eight, 10, 12 pounds at the most. This was 15-- oh, this was 75 pounds. Well, I dropped the gun in the first place. And when I pulled the trigger, the paint came out of there like a waterfall. And the worst thing, of course, happens when you paint like that-- you get runs in the paint. And that is not a spray painter. The guy told me to sit down in the corner. He went to the front office, and he says, get rid of this guy. He is not a spray painter. The guy told him in the front office-- well, you teach him how to be a spray painter. He's a refugee. He came from Shanghai. We gave him the job, and he stays. I did. I stayed there for a year and a half till my job was more or less made obsolete. And I then got a job with Shwayder Brothers-- Samsonite at the time-- where I stayed and made very good money, by the way, as a spot welder. And then lo and behold, I got a postcard in the mail that said, greetings. You will report to your friendly recruiting office. You have just been drafted-- in 1950, at the beginning of the Korean War. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well-- into the infantry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was able to-- due to my knowledge of the German language, to get out of the infantry and into the intelligence service. And I never had a chance, really, to start into the intelligence service, because I still had living relatives in Germany and in the Russian zone of Germany. So at that time, I asked the commanding officer who interviewed me at the time and says, well, what do I do now? And the guy said, well, you just do what the rest of the company does. Well, the rest of the company is going to Korea. The rest-- I'm going to start fighting the Chinese again? I didn't particularly care for that. And so I saw it real quick, and I took out of my billfold my father's business card, which at that time, we had a dental repair service, a denture repair service, a dental lab. And I said, can't you at least put me into the dental, into the Medical Corps? I'm a dental technician. He says, oh sure, we can do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he did. And I got, at that time, a permanent MOS. This is you are a dental technician, and nobody can touch you. And not only that, I was attached to the Fifth Army Carrier Central Dental Lab in San Antonio, Texas, where I spent, then, the rest of my year and a half-- it was a two-year term-- being and learning how to become a real dental technician. At the time, I thought I had a real bad time. Looking back on it, I had a wonderful time. I even had a car, a convertible. I became a Sergeant. And I had a garage for my car. In retrospect, a hell of a good time. Two years later, in 1952, just in time for Thanksgiving, I was released. I came back to Denver, and never went back to Shwayder Brothers, which they were supposed to, if I wanted to, take me back. But I-- I joined my father then. As-- as-- my father was getting sick at that time. And so I more and more took over our-- our business. Our business was a very unique service to, basically, the city of Denver. And we were the first one to start it in-- in-- in 1948 when we came to Denver in 1947, '48. We repaired false dentures that were broken in half or you broke out a tooth while you waited, right in our home. At that time, we had no competition for that, and it was a wonderful, wonderful business. Ah, the dentists didn't particularly care for that. Tried to put us out of business several times. Didn't succeed. So that's what we did. We-- we have a house, this house here at 560 Emerson Street, and lived in it here happily ever after. And in 1957, I met my wife through a blind date. And wooed her and married her, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 'bout a year later.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is she from?

speaker: interviewee
text: She is a Denverite. She is a Denverite. She is a born Stein. Her father worked for a New York Furniture store here in Denver. And we decided that Barbie, who was quite musical at one time-- she played the piano, and-- which she never liked, because her hands were much too small. In her later years, she took up drumming, became quite a proficient drummer. Never did it professionally, just-- just amateur stuff. I gave it up, because, you know, was-- kind of became a little bit hard of hearing. Had to give that up. Anyway, Barbie gave some music lessons and then became a secretary for New York-- New York Life. And then my father always believed in owning property. And so we also started with some apartment houses-- apartment houses, yes, small four-bedroom apartment houses right across from East High School. And when [? Barbie ?] and I got married, we put an addition on to our house so that we did not have to buy another house to live in. Besides, the business was downstairs in the basement. We had our business, by the way, open day and night. You broke a tooth at 9 o'clock at night, you could fix it at 9 o'clock at night. We had a real nice service there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you said we, you lived with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. Yes. We built an addition to the back of the house where Barbie and I then lived separately. All of my savings were used up to building the addition. Barbie had some savings. And so my father and Barbie went out, and were looking at another place to buy an apartment house. And so we bought a house at the corner of 14th and Race where-- this house belonged, at one time, to another senator, Ben Bezoff of Denver. He sold it to us. And it was-- a number of refugees lived in there, too. It was a share bath and a share kitchen situation, a very old house. And we cleaned floors and kitchens, and we even had to change the sheets. But hey, this is the way you get ahead in the United States. So we always had two jobs. My wife was also working as a secretary, and we had this apartment, and we cleaned the apartment. And did quite well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: We decided at an early age-- Barbie decided and I decided that we basically were scared. We really were scared of having children. We did not want the responsibility. My-- my father's and my business did-- was always, you know, kind of at the edge. There was always the dentists fighting to get us out of business. And I was scared. Are they going to succeed, and what am I going to do then? And in Barbie's mother's and sister's history was the fact that after her mother gave birth to her-- her sister, her daughter, she became sick. And after [? Shirley-- ?] that is Barbie's sister-- had one son, she was never the same afterwards, either. So Barbie said, hey, I don't think I want to risk it. So we do not have any children, and we have a very nice marriage. And we're very happy with each other, and don't regret it at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, now that it's been some 51 years since-- since the war ended, is there something that you'd like to leave as a legacy message on this tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes, I think I would. Like I said at the very beginning of this tape, you know, reading from this book, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] here I am 2,000 years later. And hopefully, I've made it. We have a comfortable home. We have lots of friends. I have hobbies. I am healthy. And we are both retired, though I work-- my hobby being model trains, I work three and a half days in a hobby shop. Which don't tell anybody-- I would work there for nothing. But they pay me a little bit, which is fine. And I enjoy my work, and I enjoy my hobbies, and I enjoy my friends. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I drive an American car. I'm sorry. I do not buy Japanese cars. I do not buy German cars. I do not feel that all of Germany were Nazis. As you know, we are here because of a German taxi driver. I'm here because a German detective or policeman risked his life and lost it to get us out of Germany. So I'm sorry. I can't say that. And I know that not all Germans were not Nazis. Yes, yes, yes. A lot of them were, and a lot of them had to protect their own skin to do what they did. But anyway, I did go back. We were invited back to Germany in 1993. I did go back in 1971 for six weeks. And I now have some very nice friends in Berlin who, when they heard that I am coming back to Berlin, took one week off to chauffeur us around, to do everything in their-- in their possibility, to-- to show us that, yes, hey, not all of us are. I'm sure that was in the back of his mind. And we are still friends. When he came to Denver this-- this summer, summer of '96, I tried to revenge myself and-- and show him as good a time as he did to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Horst Jung. And he is a manager in an electrical motor factory in-- in Berlin. He and his wife just treated us fantastic. So I guess end of story. And hopefully, the end of our wandering here in the United States with-- I fly the flag on the 4th of July, and I am an American. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I thank you very much for sharing everything that you did. OK. Mr. Chaim, now it's-- it's your chance to introduce your wife. And gives you-- I'll give you an opportunity to say anything straight to her that you'd like to say. And then Mrs. Chaim, also, if you-- you can have a couple minutes to say anything that you'd like if-- if you've learned about his experiences, and since you've been married, anything in particular that you'd like to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is my wife, Barbie. We married in 1957. And it was both our first marriage. And we are now married for 39 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't remember ever having any major fights. And we are very happy with each other. We share a lot of the same hobbies and interests, and no conflict.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm very happy with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, thank you, sweetheart. Well, I'm very, very happy with Erwin. He's a darling guy. And unlike a lot of people who might have become bitter over his experiences, I think he has learned from them. He is a better person for them. And he has a-- a generous nature. He's-- in some ways, loves America more than a lot of people that have been born here. He understands what it means to be free. And he's-- he's really quite a guy. And I think that what you're doing to allow Erwin to tell his story is extremely important for future generations so that people don't forget. And so hopefully, it will never happen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do either of you say to those who deny the occurrence of the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it happened. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: What do I say to them? Well, to each his own. There are some people who-- who cannot forget. If you remember in the early part of the story is that Mrs. Finger helped us to get a cab to get out of Berlin. At that time, Ossie Finger lost a brother in a concentration camp. He came to Denver, the United States and to Denver, and he has never forgiven. He was given an opportunity to go back to Germany, and no, no. He will not go. Mrs. Finger, I think, would have gone, and as a matter of fact, did.

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe so.

speaker: interviewee
text: She went to Europe, anyway. Yes, she did. Yes, of course she went to Germany. Yes. She had some friends over there. Ossie Finger never did. So there is one family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I must say one thing-- that the Fingers never got out of Berlin. They were hidden by friends, Christian friends, all during the war, and stayed there, and then came to the United States. And that's another story, though. So there you have, you know, no, I will not go back to Germany, and yes, I will not forgive now. We're not forgiving anything. But now this is-- this is another generation. And hopefully, all the Nazis are dead. There are still a lot of people in this world that do not exactly like the Jews. But what can you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: And some people don't believe that this-- that these events actually happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And that is why this whole thing is so important, because it did happen. It did happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you both. We will-- will now look at the pictures that you have. So we'll stop the tape and start again in a second.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Why don't you tell me who we're looking at from left to right.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my grandmother and my grandfather, I would say, taken in the 1910 or '11. My grandmother, of course, I knew personally. My grandfather passed away before I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what country this photo was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Berlin, Germany. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my mother, I would say, as a teenager. My mother's name at that time was Elsa Palnitzky. And I-- it's-- it's taken in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- do you know what year, around what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a teenager. No. I have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my father in the military service, 1914, 1918. His name is Heinrich Chaim, German pronunciation. In the United States, Henry Chaim, taken somewhere in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of Dircksenstrasse. This is the place where I spent the first two years of my life. It's an industrial district. It's four blocks away from the center of town, [INAUDIBLE]. And my grandfather had his business of making shirts to order there. I was on a balcony overlooking the elevated trains. And I think that is why model trains and anything to do with public transportation is my main hobby.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Chaims residence, the second Chaims residence in Berlin, is where the x spot is. This is where we lived from 1928 till we left Berlin. [INAUDIBLE], 20 blocks away from the center of town at the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What's that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The [? Yenowitz ?] [INAUDIBLE] is a bridge named after Mr. [? Yenowitz, ?] who, strangely enough, had some [? hot ?] [? baths ?] right there at the [? Yenowitz ?] [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's explain what we're looking at here.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father's dental practice. The second one here is the one I think he had in Buchau, Germany, the suburbs of Berlin. And in the lower picture here is my father, Heinrich Chaim at work in his dental practice mixing some cement, maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where's the lower picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The lower picture is taken at his main office, [INAUDIBLE], 10A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know what year these photos were taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: These pictures were taken, I would say, around 1933.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What are we looking at?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, are we doing? This is my father's automobile that he used as transportation to go from Berlin to Buchau, his second practice. I was roughly maybe six years old, so that would put this in around 1934. The car was [INAUDIBLE]. The license plate, as you can see, is 1A, which meant Berlin. It only had one headlight, one door on one side, one cylinder in the back. And basically speaking, it was a covered glorified motorcycle.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
